perturbation.original_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means all technical or business information disclosed by one of the Parties to another pursuant to the JRA that is identified at the time of disclosure or within thirty (30) days thereafter as being confidential and proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means all technical or business information disclosed by one of the Parties to another pursuant to the JRA that is identified at the time of disclosure or within ninety (90) days thereafter as being confidential and proprietary.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the identification period for confidential information to 90 days may conflict with New York's statute of limitations for breach of contract, which requires that the terms of the agreement are reasonably ascertainable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Extending the period to identify confidential information to 90 days after disclosure might make it difficult to prove the terms of the agreement were reasonably ascertainable within the six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract, potentially rendering the confidentiality clause unenforceable under New York law.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 90 days for identifying confidential information introduces an inconsistency that can undermine the enforceability of the confidentiality clause under New York law. The Law Explanation notes that extending the period to identify confidential information might make proving the terms of the agreement reasonably ascertainable more difficult, particularly given the six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that NY CVP § 213 mandates a six-year period for commencing actions on a contract. While the snippet does not directly address confidentiality clauses, the extended identification period complicates proving what was considered confidential within that six-year window, thus potentially contradicting the legal requirement for reasonably ascertainable terms.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire two (2) years from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to two years may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, which recognizes trade secret protection for as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_citation: Ashland Mgt. Inc. v. Janien, 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality obligation to two years may be deemed insufficient to protect legitimate trade secrets, particularly if they retain their value and confidentiality beyond that period, thereby weakening the protection afforded under New York trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Ashland Mgt. v. Janien :: 1993 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Ashland Mgt. v. Janien Annotate this Case 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993) 624 N.E.2d 1007 604 N.Y.S.2d 912 Ashland Management Incorporated, Appellant, v. C. Christopher Janien, Respondent. (And a Third-Party Action.) Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued October 14, 1993. Decided November 22, 1993. Paul, Hastings, Janofsky & Walker, New York City (Lawrence I. Weinstein, Lisa M. Buckley and Roger M. Milgrim of counsel), for appellant. Davis & Gilbert, New York City (Michael C. Lasky, Bruce M. Ginsberg and Howard Weingrad of counsel), for respondent. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges TITONE, HANCOCK, JR., BELLACOSA, SMITH and LEVINE concur. *399SIMONS, J. This litigation involves a dispute between an investment management firm and a former employee. The employee developed a mathematical model for determining investment strategy and sought unsuccessfully to sell it to the firm. A dispute arose during the negotiations and plaintiff, Ashland Management Inc., instituted this action seeking to foreclose defendant from using his model. It alleged that the model is based upon trade secrets defendant misappropriated from the firm. Defendant claims he and plaintiff had agreed upon a contract for the use of the model and that plaintiff breached the contract. Defendant, therefore, counterclaimed seeking damages. After a nonjury trial, Supreme Court found that the parties had entered into a contract and that plaintiff had breached it. Accordingly, the court awarded defendant damages of $625,000 for lost profits. It held further that the information available to defendant did not constitute a trade secret of Ashland and therefore defendant was not guilty of misappropriation. The Appellate Division, after a minor modification, affirmed the judgment. The principal legal questions presented concern the availability of damages for lost profits under the rule stated in our two decisions in Ken
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to two years, which directly contradicts the principle that trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses a trade secret dispute, it doesn't directly state the indefinite protection principle, but it does generally involve trade secret law in New York, supporting the overall context. The two-year limit weakens the protection and creates a contradiction under New York trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: 4. All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party and, upon request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return to the disclosing Party all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, or any part or reproduction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party; however, the receiving Party is not required to return CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION upon request of the disclosing Party unless the disclosing party provides written documentation of ownership.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to promptly return confidential information unless documentation is provided conflicts with the fundamental principles of contract law and fiduciary duty under New York Law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Fiduciary Duty under New York Law
perturbation.law_citation: TIAA Global Asset Management, LLC v. McMahan, 155 A.D.3d 527 (N.Y. App. Div. 2017)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York Law, a party is required to act in good faith in their contractual dealings, especially when dealing with confidential information. By removing the duty to return the confidential information, the receiving party is in a position to act contrary to the spirit of the agreement and can be seen as a breach of fiduciary duty.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the receiving party to promptly return confidential information upon request. The changed text adds a condition: the receiving party is not required to return the information unless the disclosing party provides written documentation of ownership. This creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially allowing the receiving party to misuse it before documentation is provided. This directly undermines the initial agreement regarding the confidentiality of the information. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this conflicts with the fundamental principles of contract law and fiduciary duty, and the Law Explanation suggests a potential breach of fiduciary duty under New York law, further supporting the existence of a legal contradiction. Because the changed text directly weakens the obligation to return the confidential information, a key component of the confidentiality agreement, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement is subject to the laws (excluding conflicts rules) of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Agreement is subject to the laws (excluding conflicts rules) of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware may be problematic if the parties and the subject matter of the contract have significant connections to New York, potentially leading to challenges regarding enforceability and forum selection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles Under New York Law
perturbation.law_citation: Welsbach Elec. Corp. v. MasTec N. Am., Inc., 7 N.Y.3d 624 (2006)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, courts consider various factors to determine whether to enforce a choice-of-law provision, including the location of the parties, the place of contract formation, and the subject matter of the contract. If the agreement is made and performed in New York and the parties are primarily based in New York, changing the governing law to Delaware may be disregarded if it lacks a reasonable basis and violates public policy.
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from New York to Delaware, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, creates a potential legal contradiction. While no scraped law snippets are available to directly confirm the factors New York courts consider when enforcing choice-of-law provisions, the explanations state that if the contract is made and performed in New York, and the parties are primarily based there, changing the governing law to Delaware may be disregarded if it lacks a reasonable basis and violates public policy. This situation falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction,' where the change introduces a time-sensitive obligation that violates state law, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.
